perturbation.original_text: LEADER hereby agrees to indemnify and hold EZJR, it successors, and assigns harmless from and against any and all damage or deficiency resulting from any material misrepresentation, breach of warranty or covenant, or nonfulfillment of any agreement on the part of LEADER under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: LEADER hereby agrees to consider indemnifying and holding EZJR, its successors, and assigns harmless from damage or deficiency resulting from misrepresentation, breach of warranty or covenant, or nonfulfillment of any agreement on the part of LEADER under this Agreement, if LEADER deems it reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear obligation for LEADER to indemnify EZJR. The modified text introduces ambiguity by stating that LEADER will 'consider' indemnifying EZJR 'if LEADER deems it reasonable.' This makes the indemnification obligation discretionary and weakens the protection afforded to EZJR.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 686A.030 - Unfair practices; unfair methods of competition
perturbation.law_explanation: The ambiguous indemnification clause could potentially allow LEADER to avoid fulfilling its obligations, which could be interpreted as bad faith or unfair dealing. Nevada law (NRS 686A.030) prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the business of insurance. While this isn't strictly insurance, weakening a material obligation could be seen as an attempt to avoid a fair agreement, potentially leading to legal challenges under related unfair practice statutes or common law principles regarding good faith and fair dealing in contracts.

NRS 686A.015 Jurisdiction of Commissioner; Commissioner required to establish program to investigate unfair and deceptive trade practices. NRS 686A.027 Unfair or deceptive methods, acts or practices: Knowing access or use of contractual discount of provider of health care pursuant to provider network contract without applicable contractual relationship. NRS 686A.028 Unfair or deceptive methods, acts or practices: Offering or selling policy of travel insurance that could never result in payment of claim; marketing blanket travel insurance as free. NRS 686A.070 Falsification of or knowing omissions in records or falsification of financial statements prohibited; penalty. NRS 686A.080 Defamatory statement concerning person engaged or proposing to engage in business of insurance prohibited. NRS 686A.085 Bank prohibited from requiring customers to purchase insurance from parent, subsidiary or affiliate of bank. NRS 686A.090 Boycott, coercion or intimidation tending to result in unreasonable restraint of or monopoly in business of insurance prohibited. NRS 686A.095 Cancellation or restriction of agents authority based solely on loss ratio experience prohibited in certain circumstances. NRS 686A.100 Life insurance, annuities and health insurance: Unfair discrimination prohibited. NRS 686A.105 Life insurance, annuities and health insurance: Discrimination against living organ donor prohibited. NRS 686A.110 Life insurance, annuities and health insurance: Rebates and other inducements prohibited; exceptions. NRS 686A.120 Life insurance, annuities and health insurance: Exceptions to provisions limiting discrimination, rebates and use of securities as inducements. NRS 686A.130 Property, casualty, surety and title insurance: Unfair discrimination and rebates prohibited; exceptions. NRS 686A.135 Vision care: Prohibited provisions in contract between insurer and provider; insurer required to provide list of reimbursement rates to provider before entering contract; insurer required to disclose in policy pec
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a clear obligation to indemnify with a discretionary one ('consider indemnifying... if LEADER deems it reasonable'). This weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity, potentially allowing LEADER to avoid indemnification. NRS 686A.020 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices. While the law focuses on insurance, the Law Explanation argues that weakening a material obligation could be seen as an attempt to avoid a fair agreement. The modification introduces ambiguity and discretion, making the obligation unenforceable and contradicting the principle of fair dealing.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and any matters arising out of or related to this Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and any matters arising out of or related to this Agreement will be generally guided by principles inspired by the laws of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that Nevada law governs the agreement. The modified text uses the phrase 'generally guided by principles inspired by' which weakens the governing law provision, making it ambiguous as to whether Nevada law truly applies. This could lead to disputes over which laws apply, especially if the 'principles inspired by' are not clearly defined or understood.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.1105 - Choice of law
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the choice of law provision, the modification contradicts NRS 104.1105, which relates to a parties' explicit agreement on what jurisdiction's law governs a contract. The original clause is clear on the applicable jurisdiction, however the modification introduces vagueness and disputes the applicability of Nevada laws, undercutting the original intent and standard contractual practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS: CHAPTER 104 - UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE—ORIGINAL ARTICLES [Rev. 6/29/2024 2:58:54 PM--2023] CHAPTER 104 - UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE—ORIGINAL ARTICLES ARTICLE 1 GENERAL PROVISIONS Part 1 Short Titles, Scope, Construction, Severability and Electronic Signatures and Delivery NRS 104.1101 Short titles. NRS 104.1102 Scope of Article 1. NRS 104.1103 Construction of Uniform Commercial Code to promote underlying purposes and policies; applicability of supplemental principles of law. NRS 104.1104 Construction against implied repeal. NRS 104.1105 Severability. NRS 104.1106 Use of singular and plural; gender. NRS 104.1108 Relation to Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act. Part 2 General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation NRS 104.1201 General definitions. NRS 104.1202 Notice; knowledge. NRS 104.1203 Lease distinguished from security interest. NRS 104.1204 Value. NRS 104.1205 Reasonable time; seasonableness. NRS 104.1206 Presumptions. Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules NRS 104.1301 Territorial applicability; parties power to choose applicable law. NRS 104.1302 Variation by agreement. NRS 104.1303 Course of performance, course of dealing and usage of trade. NRS 104.1304 Obligation of good faith. NRS 104.1305 Remedies to be liberally administered. NRS 104.1306 Waiver or renunciation of claim or right after breach. NRS 104.1307 Prima facie evidence by third-party documents. NRS 104.1308 Performance or acceptance under reservation of rights. NRS 104.1309 Option to accelerate at will. NRS 104.1310 Subordinated obligations. ARTICLE 2 SALES Part 1 Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter NRS 104.2101 Short title. NRS 104.2102 Scope; certain security and other transactions excluded from this article. NRS 104.2103 Definitions and index of definitions. NRS 104.2104 Definitions: Merchant; between merchants; financing agency. NRS 104.2105 Definitions: Transferability; goods; future goods; lot; commercial unit. NRS 104.2106 Definitions: Contract; agreement; contract for sale; sale; present sale; conforming to contract; termination; cancellation; hybrid transaction. NRS 104.2107 Goods to be severed from realty: Recording. Part 2 Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract NRS 104.2201 Formal requirements; statute of frauds. NRS 104.2202 Final expression: Parol or extrinsic evidence. NRS 104.2203 Seals inoperative. NRS 104.2204 Formation in general. NRS 104.2205 Firm offers. NRS 104.2206 Offer and acceptance in formation of contract.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that Nevada law governs the agreement. The changed text weakens this by stating that the agreement is 'generally guided by principles inspired by' Nevada law. This introduces ambiguity and potential disputes regarding the applicable law, contradicting NRS 104.1301, which addresses the parties' power to choose applicable law. The altered text undermines the certainty of the original agreement regarding the governing jurisdiction, making the application of Nevada law uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that after the delivery of this Agreement it or he will execute and deliver such further documents and do such further acts and things as another party may reasonably request in order to carry out the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to consider executing and delivering such further documents and performing such further acts and things as another party suggests in order to generally facilitate the perceived intent of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly mandates that each party must take necessary actions to fulfill the agreement's terms. The modified text introduces 'consider executing,' 'suggests,' and 'perceived intent,' creating ambiguity about the obligation's force. The language now makes it uncertain whether the parties must comply with requests, weakening the enforceability of further actions to uphold the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Nevada Revised Statutes (NRS) 104.1304 - Obligation of good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts NRS 104.1304, which implies an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By making further actions discretionary and based on 'perceived intent,' the modified clause undermines the principle of good faith by allowing a party to potentially avoid fulfilling actions reasonably necessary to give effect to the contract's terms.

perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to act in accordance with the agreement's terms, potentially contradicting the obligation of good faith. NRS 104.1304 states "Obligation of good faith." By changing the mandatory "will execute and deliver" to the discretionary "consider executing and delivering," the revised clause allows a party to avoid actions necessary to uphold the contract's original intent. The addition of "perceived intent" further weakens the obligation, making it subjective and potentially undermining the good faith requirement by allowing parties to act based on their own interpretations, rather than a reasonable understanding of the agreement.
